description: Technical reasoning + code tasks drawn from real Paperclip engineering: classifying run failures, fixing a token-accounting bug in the agent-scorecard SQL, implementing the prefix-routing function the MC-Compiler uses, plus harder bug-spotting (a concurrency race, a wrong-default bug from the restart-race incident) and an edge-case implementation. Deterministic checks anchor objectivity; the strict judge grades code correctness and whether subtle bugs are actually caught.

tasks.title: Classify a run failure as infra vs model fault (retry-exhaustion trap)
tasks.prompt:
A Paperclip agent run failed. Classify it for the run-recovery system.

FAILURE: the run's stderr is exactly: "Process lost -- server may have restarted". This was logged by heartbeat.ts immediately after a server restart; the agent itself never produced a turn. The run record shows this is its 4th consecutive 'process_lost' attempt and the recovery policy caps automatic retries at 3 (attempt_count=4, max_retries=3).

Classify with these rules:
- category "infra" if the root cause is environmental (server restart, process lost, OOM, network) and NOT the model's own output. category "model" only if the model produced a bad/refused/looping answer.
- retryable = true ONLY for a transient infra failure that has retry budget remaining. A failure whose attempt_count has reached or exceeded max_retries is NOT retryable, regardless of cause (it must escalate, not loop).

Return ONLY JSON: {"category": "infra"|"model", "retryable": true|false, "reason": string}. The reason must mention the exhausted retry budget. No prose outside the JSON.

tasks.title: Find the token-accounting bug in the scorecard SQL
tasks.prompt:
This SQL computes average tokens per agent run in Paperclip's agent-scorecard:

  round(avg((hr.usage_json->>'inputTokens')::numeric + (hr.usage_json->>'outputTokens')::numeric)
        FILTER (WHERE (hr.usage_json->>'outputTokens') IS NOT NULL)) AS avg_tokens

Context: under subscription billing `cost_cents` is always 0, so tokens (from usage_json) are the ONLY cost signal. Some runs store cached-prompt tokens under `cacheReadInputTokens` and `cacheCreationInputTokens`, which this expression ignores.

In 2-4 sentences: (1) state the bug, (2) explain why it matters for cost/quality-per-token comparisons, and (3) give a corrected SQL numerator expression that also counts the cache token fields. Be concise.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly identifies that cache token fields are excluded and undercount real prompt tokens, and the corrected numerator actually sums input+output+both cache fields with null-safety (e.g. COALESCE). Penalize a fix that adds cache fields but drops the existing null-safety, or that double-counts.

criteria.name: concision
criteria.guidance: Stays within ~4 sentences, no rambling.

tasks.title: Implement the MC-Compiler prefix router
tasks.prompt:
Implement this Python function exactly to spec — it is the core of Paperclip's MC-Compiler dispatch.

  def route_wake(parent_id: str, title: str, routes: list) -> str | None:
      '''routes is a list of (route_parent, title_prefix, handler) tuples.
      Return the handler for the FIRST route whose route_parent == parent_id
      AND title (after stripping leading/trailing whitespace) startswith title_prefix.
      If no route matches, return None (the caller escalates to the CTO).'''

Return ONLY the function definition in a ```python code block. No explanation.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: The function returns the first matching handler (parent equality AND stripped-title prefix match), returns None when nothing matches, and handles the tuple unpacking correctly. Penalize off-by-one ordering, missing strip, or matching on substring instead of prefix.

tasks.title: Spot the concurrency race in session-token attribution
tasks.prompt:
This Python attributes a CLI run's token usage by diffing a shared session store before and after the run. Multiple of these run CONCURRENTLY in a thread pool (different models, sometimes the SAME prompt):

  def run_and_measure(prompt, model):
      before = set(list_session_ids())          # all session ids in the shared store
      output = run_cli(prompt, model)            # creates exactly one new session
      after = list_session_ids()
      new_ids = [s for s in after if s not in before]
      sess_id = max(new_ids) if new_ids else max(after)
      tokens = export_session_tokens(sess_id)
      return output, tokens

Under concurrency this sometimes attributes the WRONG token count to a run (e.g. 200 tokens for a real call that used 19,000).

In under 120 words: (1) name the race precisely, (2) explain how two concurrent calls cause the mis-attribution, and (3) give the fix. Be specific.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly identifies that the before/after diff is not atomic: a concurrent call's new session lands in `after` too, so `new_ids` contains multiple sessions and `max(new_ids)` may pick the OTHER call's session — mis-attributing tokens. Bonus if it notes same-prompt calls can't be disambiguated by content. The fix must actually remove the race (serialize the snapshot+run with a lock, OR match the session by a per-call unique id / recorded model), not merely add retries or sort differently.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Concrete, names the exact line/mechanism, not a vague 'add thread safety'.

tasks.title: Find the wrong-default bug that fails live runs on restart

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly explains that staleThresholdMs defaults to 0, so cutoff == now, so EVERY running run (lastHeartbeatAt <= now) is treated as stale and failed immediately on startup, before it can heartbeat. The fix is to pass/raise a non-zero startup grace (e.g. 60s) so freshly-restarted runs aren't instantly reaped. Penalize answers that blame the heartbeat itself or propose just changing shouldRetry without fixing the 0-default.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Pins the 0 default and the <= cutoff comparison specifically.

tasks.title: Implement a null-safe token-cost normalizer
tasks.prompt:
Implement this Python function exactly. It normalizes a model run's raw usage dict (fields may be missing or None) into a consistent record.

  def normalize_usage(raw: dict) -> dict:
      '''Return {'input': int, 'output': int, 'total': int} where:
      - input  = (raw['inputTokens'] or 0) + (raw['cacheReadInputTokens'] or 0) + (raw['cacheCreationInputTokens'] or 0)
      - output = raw['outputTokens'] or 0
      - total  = input + output
      Any of those keys may be ABSENT or None. Never raise KeyError or TypeError.
      Always return ints (never None).'''

Return ONLY the function definition in a ```python code block. No explanation.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Handles BOTH missing keys AND explicit None safely (e.g. (raw.get('inputTokens') or 0)). A solution using raw['k'] would KeyError, and raw.get('k', 0) still breaks when the value is explicitly None — penalize those. input must sum the two cache fields too; total = input+output; all ints.

tasks.title: Diagnose an N+1 query and write the batched fix

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly identifies the N+1 pattern (one+ query per routine inside the loop -> ~2N queries) and the fix is to BATCH: fetch all routines via inArray(routines.id, ids) and all runs via inArray(runs.routineId, ids) in a bounded number of queries, then group in memory. Penalize answers that only add caching/indexes without removing the per-iteration queries, or that keep the loop.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Concrete (names inArray/IN batching + in-memory grouping), not vague 'optimize the queries'.

tasks.title: Spot the SQL injection in an OpCo admin endpoint

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly identifies SQL injection via unsanitized string interpolation of req.query.name, and gives a REAL exploit input (e.g. `' OR '1'='1` to dump all OpCos' routines, or `'; DROP TABLE routines; --` / a UNION SELECT to exfiltrate other tables). The fix MUST be parameterized/prepared queries (bind parameters / placeholders), and the answer must explain that parameterization separates code from data so the input can never be parsed as SQL. Penalize fixes that only 'escape quotes', 'validate/whitelist the input', or 'use an ORM' without parameterization as the core remedy — those are incomplete or bypassable. Penalize any answer that misses the multi-tenant cross-OpCo data-exposure impact.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Gives a concrete exploit string and the exact corrected query mechanism, not a vague 'sanitize inputs'.

tasks.title: Diagnose a flaky benchmark test
tasks.prompt:
This Python test in the model-benchmark suite passes locally but fails ~1 run in 5 in CI:

  def test_top_model_by_quality():
      results = run_bench(['claude-opus', 'codex-gpt-5.4'])   # both score quality 0.92
      ranked = sorted(results, key=lambda r: r['quality'], reverse=True)
      assert ranked[0]['model'] == 'claude-opus'

  # run_bench dispatches the two model calls CONCURRENTLY in a thread pool and
  # appends each result to `results` as it returns; both models score exactly 0.92.

The failures are non-deterministic and never reproduce when you re-run the failing case alone.

In under 120 words: (1) name precisely why this test is flaky (be specific about WHAT makes the outcome non-deterministic), (2) explain why it passes locally / in isolation but fails intermittently in CI, and (3) give the fix that makes it deterministic. Distinguish a test bug from a product bug.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly pinpoints that the two models TIE at 0.92, so sorted() (a stable sort) preserves their order in `results`, and that order is set by thread COMPLETION order, which is non-deterministic under concurrency — hence ranked[0] is sometimes codex. The flakiness depends on which thread finishes first (load-dependent, hence CI-vs-local and not reproducible in isolation). The correct framing is that the TEST is flawed (asserting a strict order on tied values), not that the product is wrong; the fix is to make the assertion deterministic — assert on the score/set, add a deterministic secondary sort key (e.g. by model name), or assert claude-opus is among the top given the tie. Penalize answers that blame timing/network flakiness generically, propose adding sleeps/retries, or 'fix' it by making run_bench sequential without addressing the tie (re-introduces flakiness the moment scores tie again under any order).

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Names the tie + stable-sort + completion-order mechanism concretely, and gives a deterministic assertion/secondary-key fix, not a vague 'add retries'.

tasks.title: Review an unsafe production DB migration

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Must catch the real production hazards: (a) the single UPDATE rewrites/locks ~40M rows in ONE transaction — long-running, bloats WAL, holds row locks, blocks/contends with the continuous heartbeat writes; (b) CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent) takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE / SHARE lock that blocks writes for the whole build; (c) on modern Postgres ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT 0 (a constant) is a fast metadata-only change, but NOT NULL combined with the immediate backfill is where the cost is — strong answers note the add-column itself is cheap and the danger is the UPDATE + index. The safe rewrite must: add the column nullable (or with the constant default), backfill in BATCHES with small transactions (e.g. by id ranges, committing per chunk), use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, and add/validate the NOT NULL constraint separately (NOT VALID then VALIDATE) — done off-peak. Penalize answers that just say 'run it at night' without batching/CONCURRENTLY, that miss the index lock, that claim ADD COLUMN itself rewrites the whole table on modern Postgres, or that propose locking the table to be 'safe'.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Names the specific Postgres locks/mechanisms (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, batched backfill, NOT VALID/VALIDATE), not a generic 'do it carefully'.

tasks.title: Find the retry-safety bug in payment processing

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly identifies the handler is NOT idempotent: on Stripe redelivery it inserts duplicate orders, grants access / emails a second license key, AND double-counts revenue (incrementRevenueCounter is the clearest non-idempotent side effect — it corrupts the revenue metric). The fix must enforce exactly-once PROCESSING: persist/check the Stripe event id (or use a UNIQUE constraint on sessionId/event id + INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) so a redelivered event is detected and the side effects are skipped — ideally do the dedup check + side effects in one transaction. Penalize fixes that only make the order insert unique but leave grantProductAccess/incrementRevenueCounter to run again on every redelivery, fixes that rely on Stripe 'not retrying' (it does), or fixes that just wrap in try/catch. A correct answer must explicitly cover the non-DB side effects (email/grant + counter), not just the insert.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Names event-id dedup / unique constraint / ON CONFLICT and explicitly addresses the counter + access-grant side effects, not a vague 'handle duplicates'.

tasks.title: Fix the timezone off-by-one in the daily report window

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Must catch BOTH bugs. (1) Timezone: the window is built in UTC, but the day is meant in Europe/Dublin (UTC+1 in summer), so the window is shifted 1h — a run at 00:30 LOCAL is 23:30 UTC the PREVIOUS day, so it falls into the prior day's report (and the last hour of UTC bleeds in from the wrong day). The correct window in UTC is 2026-06-19T23:00:00Z up to 2026-06-20T23:00:00Z (i.e. local midnight to next local midnight converted to UTC, ideally via the IANA zone so DST is handled, not a hardcoded +1). (2) Boundary off-by-one: using lte(end) with 23:59:59 drops anything in the final second (23:59:59.x–23:59:59.999) and is fragile; the correct pattern is a HALF-OPEN interval gte(start) AND lt(nextDayStart). Penalize answers that fix only the timezone but keep the inclusive 23:59:59 end (or vice versa), that hardcode +1 without noting DST/IANA, or that get the shift DIRECTION wrong (e.g. claim the local run moves to the next day).

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: States the exact UTC window (…19T23:00 to …20T23:00), the +1 offset/DST caveat, and the half-open lt() boundary — not a vague 'handle timezones'.
